data.title: NUDV: ESG Goals Met But Quality And Dividend Screens Fall Short
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Nuveen ESG Dividend ETF (BATS:NUDV) consists primarily of large- and mid-cap dividend-paying U.S. stocks that pass specific and transparent environmental, social, and governance screens. With a 0.26% expense ratio and a 2.55% 30-day SEC yield, NUDV can reasonably support ESG investors' income goals, and my analysis also suggests it has decent capital appreciation potential. In this article, I will compare NUDV with four other dividend-oriented ETFs, and while NUDV falls short due to a suboptimal screening process for quality and dividends, it's not a bad choice at all for ESG investors. I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

NUDV Overview

Strategy Discussion

NUDV tracks the Nuveen ESG USA High Dividend Yield Index, which its website notes "aims to represent the performance of a set of securities with high dividend income and quality characteristics while maximizing the exposure to positive environmental, social and governance factors as well as exhibiting lower carbon exposure than the MSCI USA Index." Furthermore, as noted in the Index methodology document, the Index selects at least 300 securities initially, subject to the dividend sustainability, quality, and yield screens below.

Afterward, the Index applies optimization to remove securities with negligible weights (i.e., less than 0.1%) and those involved in controversial business activities. These include alcohol, tobacco, gambling, nuclear power, weapons, and firearms to reduce the number of eligible constituents. Today, NUDV holds 95 stocks, though components are reviewed quarterly in February, May, August, and November.

Peer Group Summary

In this article, I will compare NUDV with four dividend ETFs, as follows:

  1. Xtrackers S&P Dividend Aristocrat Screened ETF (SNPD)
  2. iShares Core Dividend ETF (DIVB)
  3. Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)
  4. Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV)

Between these four peers, we have a pretty good sample to work with. SNPD is obviously the closest comparator from an ESG perspective, though DIVB has turned into a solid and well-diversified contender since its strategy change on December 16, 2022. I think it's safe to say that SCHD needs no introduction, given its $68 billion in AUM and popularity on Seeking Alpha. Lastly, I wanted to include CGDV in the mix as a lower-yielding option that's delivered excellent returns since its launch over three years ago. For a deeper dive into that ETF, please feel free to read my review from earlier this month.

Before we get into the analysis, I just want to quickly highlight how NUDV's posted 5.70% trailing yield includes a $1.0236 per share short-term gains distribution from December 2024. As shown below, that gain is irregular, and excluding it reveals a trailing dividend yield on the income portion only of just 2.05%. You should always review official source documents like an ETF's website, fund facts, and prospectus before buying an ETF, but in this case, I was tipped off when seeing the large discrepancy between NUDV's four-year average yield and its trailing yield (2.73% vs. 5.70%).

NUDV Analysis

Performance Snapshot

Since its launch on September 27, 2021, NUDV has delivered a respectable 27.57% total return, which was about 9% better than SCHD but 8% behind DIVB. Unfortunately, SNPD and CGDV don't have sufficient track records to be included in the chart below.

For greater context, NUDV's three-year total returns rank #39/98 among the large and mid-cap value ETFs I track. While this ranking is solid, it still trailed several of the better-known funds like DGRO, VYM, and IWD over this period while essentially matching the performance of DJD. There are plenty more examples, too, but the takeaway here is that NUDV has not demonstrated its dividend sustainability, quality, and yield screens work from a total returns perspective. Instead, ESG is the main attraction.

Sector Allocations and Size Distributions

The following two tables highlight NUDV's sector allocation and size segment distribution differences with SNPD, DIVB, SCHD, and CGDV. The first table reveals that NUDV has excellent sector diversification, with at least 3% allocated to all 11 sectors. No sector contributes to more than 23% of the fund, and except for SNPD, it appears to be the best diversified at this level.

The second table shows each ETF's size segment distributions using the following breakpoints:

  • Giant (Top 40% Cumulative Market Cap): Above $350B
  • Large (Next 30% Cumulative Market Cap): $71-350B
  • Mid (Next 20% Cumulative Market Cap): $12-71B
  • Small (Next 7% Cumulative Market Cap): $3-12B
  • Micro (Bottom 3% Cumulative Market Cap): Below $3B

Using these definitions, NUDV overweights mid-cap stocks by 10.64% but underweight small-caps by 8.22%, but for the most part, it's reasonably in line with SNPD, DIVB, and SCHD. In contrast, CGDV is the outlier, with 33% allocated to "Giant" stocks with market caps above $350 billion and a weighted average market cap of $795 billion, compared to just $126 billion for NUDV. Clearly, they're two different funds despite being classified in the same large-cap value category, per Morningstar.

Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for NUDV's top 25 holdings, which comprise 48.83% of the portfolio. That concentration level is about the same as SNPD and DIVB and much less than SCHD and CGDV, so right away, we can call NUDV a pretty well-diversified ETF, perhaps worthy of a "core" holding label for ESG investors. However, total returns matter, too, so have a quick look at the other summary metrics at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

Dividends: 2.70% Index Yield, 2.44% Expected Yield

The best way to predict an ETF's dividend yield is to calculate its weighted average dividend yield, also known as the Index yield for Index-based ETFs, and subtract the expense ratio. This method is more accurate because it reflects the fund's actual holdings and the income the fund will receive from its underlying holdings moving forward. In contrast, the ETF's trailing yield reflects what the fund used to hold, and depending on the reconstitution frequency and turnover rates, this figure is sometimes irrelevant.

Moving on, when we apply the calculation to each ETF, we get the following:

  • NUDV: 2.70% - 0.26% = 2.44%
  • SNPD: 3.38% - 0.15% = 3.33%
  • DIVB: 2.92% - 0.05% = 2.87%
  • SCHD: 4.03% - 0.06% = 3.97%
  • CGDV: 1.79% - 0.33% = 1.46%

NUDV's 2.44% expected dividend yield is below average, and after scanning through the top holdings list, most selections are in the 2-3% range. Meanwhile, SCHD's top holdings include several stocks yielding above 4%, like Chevron (CVX) and Merck & Co (MRK), while CGDV has hardly any and is instead led by three low-yielding Technology stocks: Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Nvidia (NVDA). Again, while these ETFs all fall into the same large-cap value category, there's quite a range in style, so I think it's important not to paint them all with the same brush.

NUDV: Good Earnings Growth, Slightly Elevated Valuation

NUDV's holdings offer a solid 9.72% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate, which is about 7% better than their three-year historical earnings growth rates. This large difference raises some concern, but it's common to all five ETFs, with differences ranging between 5-9%. For example, SNPD, DIVB, and SCHD have estimated growth rates that are 5.91%, 6.55%, and 4.96% higher, respectively. However, NUDV's advantage is that at least its historical earnings growth rate is positive, which is needed to support dividend growth.

NUDV trades at 16.30x forward earnings, which, I think, is slightly elevated, given how it allocates nearly one-quarter to the traditionally cheap Financials sector. After converting each ETF components' Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, I found that NUDV was behind SNPD, DIVB, and SCHD, though much better than CGDV. Again, this points to CGDV being pretty close to the "blend" segment on the value-blend-growth style spectrum, while SCHD is firmly on the value end. Value scores are as follows:

  • NUDV: 3.77/10
  • SNPD: 4.64/10
  • DIVB: 4.22/10
  • SCHD: 5.24/10
  • CGDV: 2.45/10

NUDV: Quality Could Be Better

One of the most disappointing finds was that NUDV's quality features didn't stand out, despite it being one of the screens used in the selection process. Revisiting that screen, the methodology states that it only excludes securities with a negative quality z-score, which, in simple terms, means excluding companies with a below-average composite quality score that combines a company's ROE, earnings variability, and leverage. To be sure, the screen serves some purpose, but NUDV's 22.66% weighted average ROE is about 3% and 7% lower than CGDV and SCHD, respectively, and about the same as DIVB. NUDV is also a little low on net margins and ROA, and like its value features, these are nicely reflected in the following sector-adjusted profit scores that I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades:

  • NUDV: 8.06/10
  • SNPD: 7.71/10
  • DIVB: 8.53/10
  • SCHD: 9.11/10
  • CGDV: 9.19/10

Notable Exclusions: Is ESG Worth It?

While I'm satisfied NUDV is a better ESG dividend fund than SNPD, I'm not confident it's better than DIVB, and SCHD and CGDV look to be solid choices depending on where on the value-blend-growth spectrum you want to land. That begs the question - should you abandon your ESG objectives in favor of a fundamentally stronger fund? I think it depends how much, and to answer that, let's look at the top 25 holdings common to each ETF and find out exactly what NUDV excludes:

I've bolded the stocks that NUDV excludes, and in many cases, the reasons are obvious. These include oil stocks like Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM), weapons stocks like RTX Corp. (RTX), and tobacco stocks like Altria Group (MO). However, NUDV excludes many lower-yielding stocks that have delivered strong returns over the last five years, including JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Broadcom (AVGO).

In my view, the Index's yield requirement (1.3x the yield of the Parent Index) doesn't serve shareholders well, as these stocks get removed even though keeping them would have only a negligible impact on the portfolio's yield. Other funds, like the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV), have demonstrated that you can still produce a decent dividend yield even with low-fielders NVDA, MSFT, AAPL, JPM, and AVGO as its top five holdings. This might not be practical for the highest-yielding funds like SCHD, but NUDV's yield isn't that high, so I think Nuveen should focus more on the net portfolio yield rather than setting a minimum requirement for each individual component.

Investment Recommendation

In this article, I found that NUDV has a moderate 2.44% expected dividend yield and a solid 9.72% one-year estimated earnings growth rate but also has disappointing value and quality features. As you might expect from an ESG fund, NUDV excludes stocks in the tobacco, oil, and weapons industries, but its greatest headwind is its selection process itself. Specifically, its quality screen doesn't remove enough low-quality stocks, and its yield screen excludes fundamentally strong stocks, many of which have delivered excellent returns over the last five years. Therefore, while NUDV isn't a bad ETF by any stretch, I'm only comfortable assigning it an average "hold" rating. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Meet The 899-Ton Elephant In U.S. Tax Reform
data.description:
It’s often said there are only two certainties in life: death and taxes. However, the tax landscape may become somewhat murkier, as the recently passed U.S. House budget bill may potentially lead to some non-U.S. investors paying more taxes than previously anticipated. Although policy uncertainty remains elevated, we think it’s worth discussing the potential impact of this tax measure, and why taking a disciplined approach remains the best course of action.

Tax Pro Quo

In May, the U.S. House of Representatives passed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBA)—the budget bill for this congressional cycle. Although the bill had more than 1,000 pages of text, there was an “899-ton” elephant in the room that caught the eye of many non-U.S. investors: a measure authorizing retaliatory taxes.

Section 899 of the OBBA aims to penalize foreign countries that put in place what the U.S. views as “unfair”, discriminatory taxes against U.S. corporations. The provision introduces gradually higher U.S. withholding taxes on dividends—and potentially other forms of passive income—to residents of these countries. This amounts to a “tax pro quo”: tax our companies, and we’ll tax your investors.

In the House version, withholding tax rates would rise by 5 percentage points annually, up to a maximum of 20 points above current levels. The total withholding tax rates are also capped at 41%. The Senate version would see a rate increase of up to 15 percentage points. These withholding tax increases would likely apply to dividend income, though may not apply to interest income from investing in U.S. treasuries or U.S. corporate bonds. The Senate version of the bill specifically codifies this exception.

In or Out?

As written, the bill gives the U.S. government a fair degree of flexibility in terms of defining what constitutes an “unfair” tax. However, there are some specific inclusions and exclusions.

  • Specific Inclusion: Digital services taxes (DST) are specifically listed as a tax that would be subject to this provision. Canada, the UK and many of the countries in the European Union currently have a version of this tax, which means they could face these retaliatory taxes. In addition, any country that applies an Undertaxed Profits Rule (UTPR) tax would also be subject to this provision, putting countries like Australia on the list of potential targets too.
  • Specific exclusion: Taxes on sales. For example, the Canadian Goods and Services Tax and the European Value Added Tax would not be deemed “unfair” taxes.

The broad reach of this rule means many of the U.S.' closest trading partners and allies may be affected, creating uncertainty for a wide range of investors.

Dividend Downside

If Section 899 is enacted and a country is designated as discriminatory, the most direct consequence for its investors would be a reduction in after-tax returns on U.S. dividends. As the table below shows, withholding taxes could rise substantially. Note that for the third column, we assume the 41% cap on the total withholding tax rate contained in the House version holds.

However, the tax rate is only one part of the equation. We also need to consider the tax base. Relative to other markets, U.S. companies tend to pay lower dividends —for instance, the dividend yield on the S&P 500 Index was only 1.4% as of mid-June. Accordingly, a global equity investor holding a market cap-weighted portfolio and subject to an additional 15% U.S. dividend withholding tax (as per the Senate version) would only experience a return drag of roughly 0.13%.1 This is not ideal, but unlikely to alter the overall investment case for U.S. equity exposure in a diversified global portfolio.

Investors with a concentrated investment in higher dividend-yielding investments (like real estate investment trusts or utility companies) could see a bigger impact. These investors could potentially face a return drag of one full percentage point2, and may need to reassess the after-tax effectiveness of their investment strategy.

While we believe the exception for interest income would most likely hold in the final version of the bill, the potential withholding tax rate increases would create an even bigger return drag if the exemption is not maintained.

Beyond mechanical calculations of increased withholding taxes, the broader concern is behavioural. Section 899 may reinforce a growing worry among some foreign investors that the U.S. laws/regulations may be changing in a less predictable manner. Even if the direct tax effect appears modest, these perception concerns could alter the demand for U.S. assets.

This sentiment shift could prove more consequential over time. With U.S. stocks already expensive compared to historical averages and international peers, even a slight drop in demand from foreign investors could encourage more people to shift money into non-U.S. markets. Reallocation could be consequential, as non-U.S. investors account for nearly 20% of U.S. stock ownership3.

Down the line, this reallocation away from the U.S. could make it more expensive for U.S. companies to raise money—particularly in capital-intensive, high-dividend sectors. Over time, this may lead firms to scale back investment or expansion plans, potentially weighing on longer-term economic growth and dampening America’s competitive edge as a magnet for global capital.

Not the Final Word

Although Section 899 is often described by the media as a “revenge tax”, we believe that—similar to tariffs—this provision may be designed as a bargaining tool to discourage tax practices (like digital services taxes) that could raise tax bills for U.S. corporations.

In fact, the specific exclusion of sales taxes from the definition of unfair taxes may have been designed to help reach a compromise. That’s because it’s probably easier for Europe or Canada to repeal their digital services taxes rather than outright abolish their sales taxes. If the taxes covered by Section 899 are removed, investors won’t have to pay extra withholding taxes.

The final version of the budget bill could also be watered down further as lawmakers consider the potentially negative consequences of this rule.

Bargaining Chip

At face value, Section 899 should not be a major concern for most investors. The increase in withholding tax would likely be gradual and wouldn’t take effect before 2026-27. Even if fully implemented, the drag on returns is unlikely to be material for diversified portfolios unless interest income is also subject to withholdings. And given that members of the U.S. administration have expressed a desire to keep government borrowing costs low, we do not believe the government would subject interest income to these additional withholdings, as that could run counter to its objectives. The intent behind the measure appears less about retribution and more about leverage—a negotiating tool to pressure allies rather than a permanent shift in U.S. tax policy. In addition, there’s a real possibility the rule is softened, delayed, or never fully enacted.

But while the immediate financial impact may be limited, a longer-term and more subtle risk may be emerging. Section 899 contributes to a pattern of unilateral policymaking that increasingly blurs the line between economic strategy and diplomacy. And while we believe the U.S. is likely to retain its strategic advantage in technological innovation and new business formation, the competitive gap could gradually narrow over time should investors start rethinking their portfolio allocation.

In that sense, Section 899 may not be the heaviest burden. The true elephant in the room isn’t the provision itself, but what it signals about the future role of the U.S. in the global system. And that’s an elephant worth watching—however slowly it moves.

1 Assuming the U.S. stock market has a weight of 65% in global equities and a dividend yield of 1.4%

2 Illustrative scenario based on a 5% dividend yield

3 Source: Federal Reserve Z.1 (Q1 2025), U.S. Treasury

Disclosure

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

data.title: Compañía de Minas Buenaventura: Silver Shines, But Transition Still Ongoing
data.description:
Compañía de Minas Buenaventura (NYSE:BVN) delivers mixed results, let's say Q1 2025, financials were solid, but operationally it’s still a company in transition. The silver segment is finally doing some real lifting, thanks to the Yumpag mine hitting full stride, while gold and base metals continue to drag. That said, management did what it could with the cards dealt: Cerro Verde threw off a hefty dividend, San Gabriel is inching toward completion, and the balance sheet is strong enough to keep things moving without much financial stress.

Still, there are issues. Gold production fell sharply, and copper volumes slid, with El Brocal’s open pit closure weighing heavily. And even though prices helped offset the volume story, this isn’t a perfect quarter, neither I believe the following will be better. Not terrible, not great, just a decent checkpoint for a company rebuilding momentum, but it will take a while for it to do so.

Q1 2025 Results

Strong Q1 2025 financial results, largely on the back of silver prices and a timely ramp at Yumpag. Total revenues hit $307.7 million, a 25% jump from the same period last year. That kind of topline growth in this environment isn’t easy to come by, especially with major operations like El Brocal scaling down and gold production falling off a cliff. But strong silver pricing and, more importantly, more silver volume helped fill the gap here.

Operating income doubled to $93.9 million, up from $46.9 million in Q1 2024. That kind of margin expansion wasn’t just luck. Cost control looks intact, and higher grades at Yumpag certainly helped. The real performance, though, was EBITDA from direct operations, which landed at $126.3 million, up 34% YoY. And when you include equity affiliates, Cerro Verde, total EBITDA rose to $251.1 million, a 35% jump YoY. That’s a big number. Cerro Verde alone contributed $59.2 million in equity income, up from $35.8 million a year ago, driven by stronger copper and molybdenum prices, even as its own copper output slightly declined.

At the bottom line, net income more than doubled, reaching $140.1 million compared to $61.4 million a year ago. That goes to $0.55/share, more than double last year’s $0.24/share. This was also helped by a $13.2 million provisional price adjustment, up from $3.3 million last year. Think of that as a mark-to-market tailwind, helpful, but not something you want to count on every quarter, and I wouldn't myself. Still, it helped this one look better, but as mentioned, do not assume this might repeat.

The real pricing tailwinds came from the metals market. Silver averaged $32.22/oz, from $23.44/oz in Q1 2024. Copper prices also climbed, averaging $9,292/ton, up from $8,258/ton. These things mattered, especially since volumes in gold and base metals fell, so if the prices correct as the cycle does its thing, due to volume drop, we might see worse quarterly results.

As I mentioned earlier, a good chunk of this financial strength comes down to the company finally monetizing more of its silver position. Yumpag’s ramp-up came just in time to ride silver’s pricing surge. In other words, volume met price at just the right moment and that is very good, as we might see this type of silver performance in near future. This saved the quarter. Because if you dig into the production tables, there’s weakness almost everywhere else, gold down, copper down, zinc and lead down significantly. Without silver, this quarter would’ve been very bad.

Management is clearly aware of this. They called out Yumpag and Cerro Verde as the two big contributors. And they weren’t wrong. But the dependence on these two operations means BVN’s Q1 isn’t as diversified or balanced as it may look at first glance. There’s execution risk in here, particularly on the gold side, where mines like Tambomayo and Orcopampa underdelivered due to sequencing and lower grades.

Still, cash flow is solid. And the company appears positioned well enough to absorb the softness in some areas. The dividend increase (discussed later) and ongoing debt reduction wouldn’t be happening if management didn’t feel at least some confidence in the earnings trajectory.

Risks

Let’s be clear, this was a solid quarter, but it’s not a clean story. BVN has a lot going on under the hood, and not all of it is working smoothly. If you strip out silver and the Cerro Verde dividend, the rest of the operation looks pretty uneven.

Start with gold production, which was just plain weak. Tambomayo fell off a cliff (down 67%), Orcopampa was scaled back "on purpose," and Coimolache underdelivered. Even La Zanja only looks good because expectations were basically zero. Management says the second half will be better, but they’re leaning hard on sequencing and leach pad expansions to save the year. If grades don’t cooperate or timelines slip, they miss. That’s not catastrophic, but it would kill momentum and probably push shares into consolidation, or down.

El Brocal’s transition also adds a layer of noise. It used to be a decent poly-metallic asset, now it’s just copper with lower volumes and no by-products to pad the numbers. Zinc and lead are gone, and silver dropped 50%. Until the underground mine proves it can carry its weight, Brocal is more of a drag than a driver. And copper CAS is up 20%, not ideal if prices soften, which they will eventually.

Speaking of which, costs are inching higher across the board. CAS for gold at both Tambomayo and Coimolache is now over $2,000/oz. That’s fine when spot gold is over $3000, but if prices dip even a little, margins get tight fast. BVN can stomach a few quarters like that, but it chips away at the buffer.

And then there’s San Gabriel, the company’s "future"(I know I am sarcastic, but let's be real). It’s 79% built and looks okay for a Q4 start, but they’ve already raised the capex budget, and I have seen this movie before. Until it’s online and actually producing, there’s always risk of delay, budget creep, or technical issues. If it misses even by a couple months, the 2025 exit rate looks a lot less attractive.

Catalysts

Despite the rough spots, BVN does have a few solid things working in its favor, but I would call them near term catalysts that could push if they land right, not really for long term.

First up, San Gabriel, yes I know I just said it's a risk. It’s 79% built, most of the processing plant is already installed, and if the company can actually hit its target for first gold by Q4 2025, that would be a big deal. We're talking about ~120,000 ounces of annual production once it ramps, pretty meaningful considering current gold output is trending lower. The project’s already over budget, but if they deliver even close to schedule, the market will probably give them some credit and will price it in by the year end.

Second, Cerro Verde keeps paying. BVN pulled in $49 million in dividends in April alone. That’s a huge boost to free cash flow and gives them breathing room for capex or even another dividend raise. As long as copper and moly prices hold up, this stake is a reliable income stream, arguably more dependable than a few of their own mines. But I fear that this is just part of the cycle, and it might not hold, so that is why my rating is HOLD, this company can become a nightmare on cycle's end.

Third, silver strength. With Yumpag now fully online, BVN has finally rebalanced toward silver at the right time. Prices jumped to over $32/oz this quarter, and if that strength sticks, even moderately, it could keep earnings elevated. Yumpag has room to keep delivering, and if grades hold, it could quietly become one of BVN’s most important assets.

Also, I noticed that it is worth watching the reserve growth. The latest reserve update added 61M oz of silver, 482k oz of gold, and 253k tonnes of copper. Some of this came from infill drilling, some from project updates, but either way, it shows they’re not standing still.

Again, I will say that none of these are boosting changes, but they’re worth tracking. If execution lines up, these could change the tone of BVN’s story heading into 2026.

Valuation

On earnings, BVN’s P/E (Non-GAAP TTM) sits at 8.84, cheaper than IAG (13.67) and EGO (13.08), but more expensive than EQX. Looking forward, it drops to 9.14x FY2 earnings, which isn’t bad at all if they can hit gold guidance in the second half. Still, this isn’t a deep-value stock, you’re paying a fair price for execution that still needs to play out.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) is 10.02, again not outrageous, but not compelling either. EGO and IAG are down around 4–5x. If you want leverage to a gold price spike, they’re arguably more geared. But BVN has something they don’t, a stable cash machine in Cerro Verde and a working silver engine at Yumpag. That probably justifies the premium, at least partially, we can say that.

Price to book is 1.22, basically in line with IAG and just a bit above EGO (1.06). Nothing flashy here. Investors are still paying close to asset value, which suggests the market is cautiously optimistic, but not overly confident either.

One thing worth pointing out: Price to Cash Flow (TTM) is 8.63, which stacks up okay versus peers like IAG (8.78) and much better than EQX (10.94) or TFPM (19.90). For a miner with multiple cash-generating assets, that’s not a bad place to be, especially since BVN isn’t blowing money on dumb acquisitions right now.

In short, valuation doesn’t give you a strong reason to buy or sell, so that is why my rating is HOLD. BVN is priced like a company that has work to do, but could get a different rating if San Gabriel lands well and production stabilizes.

Summary

Q1 2025 looked strong on paper, revenues up, EBITDA solid, net income doubled, but dig a little, and it's clear BVN is still a work in progress. Silver saved them. Cerro Verde paid the bills. Everything else was either flat or down. Gold production? Weak. Brocal? In transition and not carrying its weight yet. Costs? Rising in the wrong places.

That said, the balance sheet is clean, San Gabriel is moving forward (though not without some cost inflation), and the reserve update was solid across gold, silver, and copper. Management isn’t doing anything dumb, and they’ve clearly shifted toward internal execution over aggressive expansion, which is the right call.

If San Gabriel hits its timeline and gold production rebounds, maybe there's upside. But we’re not there yet. Right now, the setup looks balanced, some progress, some risk, fair valuation. HOLD.

data.title: Perion Network: 2025 Is A Transformative Year, But Stock Is Not That Cheap
data.description:
In my previous article about Perion Network Ltd. (NASDAQ:PERI), I noted that the use of AI technologies, the stock repurchases, and ongoing restructuring operations could accelerate the demand for the stock. In the last a few months, we saw an increase in the stock price, and PERI delivered a new quarterly report. I believe that there is a lot of new information to offer a new update. The recent words from management about the fact that PERI could go through a transformative year in 2025, the new guidance, and the synergies from the acquisition of Greenbids seem quite beneficial. With that, I did not appreciate the recent decline in revenue in the last quarter. I incorporated all this new information in may DCF models, and offered a new price target. PERI looks cheap, but not as cheap as in the past. In my opinion, it is worth monitoring the stock.

PERI: 2025 Is Expected To Be A Transformative Year, And New Acquisition

PERI presents its business model as a platform to reach all advertising channels thanks to advanced AI technologies a well as to deliver relevant ads.

I believe that it is a great moment for revisiting the company's business model. In the most recent quarterly press release, the company noted that the year 2025 is expected to be a transformative year thanks to new R&D efforts around AI technologies and recent acquisitions. In my view, the investors who really understand where PERI is going will be making money.

I believe that 2025 will be a year of transformation for Perion, and we are gradually adding the necessary components to our existing capabilities through responsible acquisitions and focusing our R&D efforts on AI-enabled solutions. Source: Quarterly Press Release

It is also worth noting that the company raised its 2025 revenue guidance because of the recent acquisition of Greenbids, which is expected to bring new synergies. In this regard, PERI provided the following information to investors in the most recent press release.

As a result of the organic growth we delivered in the first quarter, along with the highly synergistic acquisition of Greenbids, we are raising our full year 2025 revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance. Source: Quarterly Press Release

With that about the expectations for 2025, PERI did not deliver a beneficial quarterly report. Net sales decreased as a result of changes implemented by Microsoft Bing in 2024, which I discussed in my previous article. In my view, considering the number of years PERI operated in the industry, I believe that management knows how to redesign the business model and marketing strategies to further deliver revenue growth.

Revenue decreased by 43% to $89.3 million in the first quarter of 2025 from $157.8 million in the first quarter of 2024. Advertising Solutions revenue decreased 8% year-over-year, accounting for 78% of total revenue, primarily due to a 28% decrease in our Web channel, partially offset by 80% increase in Digital Out of Home revenue and a 31% year-over-year increase in CTV revenue. Search Advertising revenue decreased by 76% year-over-year, accounting for 22% of revenue, following the previously announced changes implemented by Microsoft Bing in 2024. Source: Quarterly Press Release

In the last quarterly report, PERI noted lower than expected EPS GAAP of -$0.19, and quarterly revenue of $89 million. The company is also trading at 10x Non-GAAP FWD earnings, and 2.85x FWD EBITDA. I believe that PERI looks cheap.

Stock Repurchases Could Bring A Substantial Demand For The Stock

In my opinion, directors inside PERI understand that the company is quite undervalued. Clearly, the recent share repurchase program authorized in 2025 indicates that the company is willing to accelerate the demand for the stock. According to the last press release, PERI acquired 9 million shares for about $79.3 million. It means that PERI acquired shares at $8.9 per share, which is not far from the current price mark.

In March 2025, Perion’s Board of Directors authorized a $50 million expansion of the previously authorized share repurchase program of $75 million of its outstanding shares, to a total of $125 million. Source: Quarterly Press Release

As of May 12, 2025, the Company repurchased a total of 9 million shares, at a total amount of $79.3 million. Source: Quarterly Press Release

Best Case Scenario

Under my best case scenario, I assumed that we may see further repurchase of shares. I also assumed that the company will most likely redesign the business model with new AI technologies.

I included a WACC of 7.8%, which is not far from the figures used by other analysts out there. Other analysts used cost of equity of 8.7%-11%, and cost of debt of around 7%.

Furthermore, I used FCF of $62 million in 2025 and $42 million in 2035. Moreover, with a WACC of 7.8%, net debt of -$351 million, 44.9 million shares, and terminal multiple of 5x, I obtained an implied fair price of $18 per share.

Risks From Search Services And Concentration

PERI has to work with internet browsers. According to information delivered by PERI, the number of browsers in the market appears to be limited. A few browsers are responsible for a significant portion of the market share. It means that PERI may not have a lot of negotiating power, and may have to accept new changes from these few players. The company provided a few details about the browser market in the last annual report.

As we provide our services through the internet, we rely on our ability to work with different internet browsers. The internet browser market is extremely concentrated with Google’s Chrome, Apple’s Safari, Microsoft Edge and Mozilla’s Firefox, accounting for over 94% of the desktop browser market as of December 2024, with Google’s Chrome alone accounting for over 65.54%, based on StatCounter reports as of February 2025. Source: 20-F

In the most recent history, large tech groups limited the number of options for partners such as PERI. PERI does not seem to have a lot of freedom to offer certain services to clients. In the future, the company may suffer from further restrictions on search services. Under certain conditions, I believe that the company's profitability may deteriorate, and future free cash flow margin growth may decline. As a result, we could see stock price declines.

In the past years, internet browser providers such as Google and Microsoft made changes and updated their policies and technology in general, and specifically those relating to changes of search settings. Each such change limits and constrains our ability to offer or change search properties. In addition, the desktop operating system market is very concentrated as well, with Microsoft Windows accounting for over 70.54% of the market in February 2025 and Apple MacOS accounting for more than 15.77% of the market, based on StatCounter reports as of February 2025. In June 2018, Google limited the ability to install Chrome browser extensions exclusively through the Chrome Web Store. Source: 20-F

Risks From Data Collection

PERI receives a substantial amount of information about the way users engage with adds. The company appears to be in need of this information to understand the results of marketing campaigns. According to the last annual report, the company also receives personal data about the user, which appears necessary for ad placement. In the future, new regulations may control a bit better the way companies deal with information from consumers. Personal data could become more expensive, or the dealing with certain information could even become illegal. Under such case scenario, PERI would most likely suffer substantially. The company could see decline in its net income growth and FCF growth.

In many cases, when we deliver an advertisement, we are able to collect certain data, including personal data, about the content and placement of the ad, the relevancy of such ad to a user and the interaction of the user with the ad, such as whether the user viewed or clicked on the ad or watched a video. Our ability to collect, use, maintain and otherwise process such data is crucial. Source: 20-F

Risks From OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Microsoft’s Copilot, and Google’s Gemini

The emergence of AI could also bring a number of problems and uncertainties. These days, search engines seem to incorporate tools such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Microsoft’s Copilot, and Google’s Gemini, which may change the way individuals conduct research. In my opinion, if individuals reduce the amount of research that they conduct thanks to the new tools, perhaps the amount of information that PERI receives could decline. Again, personal information and information about search activities could decline, which may damage the company's profitability. There is plenty of information about these matters in the last annual report, however I would invite readers to have a look at the lines below.

Our search business model relies significantly on third-party search engine results, predominantly those provided by Microsoft Bing and Yahoo. This dependence exposes our operations to the evolving landscape of AI technologies, including OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Microsoft’s Copilot and Google’s Gemini, which are and increasingly becoming integral to search engines. The introduction of ChatGPT in November 2022, along with the launch of other AI platforms such as Gemini (formerly known as Bard) by Alphabet Inc., Google’s parent company, Claude by Anthropic, and Grok by X, are anticipated to result in the development of tools that could enhance competition in the advertising technology industry and reduce barriers to entry. Source: 20-F

Risks From Failed Acquisitions

PERI acquired a significant number of companies in the past, and I would expect further acquisition of companies in the future. Acquiring other companies brings a number of problems. First, there is the fact that employees inside PERI may be distracted, and may not appreciate inorganic growth. They may leave the company. Besides, PERI may also pay too much for targets, and could suffer from goodwill impairments, which could deteriorate the company's book value per share. Furthermore, in my opinion, investors out there may not appreciate new acquisitions. They may wonder why individuals inside the organization are not capable of making new inventions. They have to acquire other companies to grow.

We acquired Content IQ LLC, in January 2020, Pub Ocean Media UK Limited in July 2020, Vidazoo Ltd., in October 2021 and Hivestack Technologies Inc. (“Hivestack”), in December 2023 and we may continue to acquire complementary products, technologies or businesses. Seeking and negotiating potential acquisitions to a certain extent diverts our management’s attention from other business concerns and is expensive and time-consuming. It is not certain that the negotiations with respect to potential acquisition may lead to the consummation of such acquisition. Acquisitions expose us and our business to unforeseen liabilities or risks associated with the business or assets acquired or with entering new markets. Source: 20-F

Worst Case Scenario Using Previous Risks

Under my worst case scenario, I forecasted FCF of $38 million in 2025 and $8 million in 2035. Moreover, with a WACC of 7.8%, 44.9 million shares, net debt of -$351 million, and terminal multiple of 5x, I obtained the implied fair price of $11 per share.

In Conclusion

Perion Network expects the year 2025 to be quite a transformative period, and the recent acquisition of Greenbids appears to bring a large amount of synergies. With that bring said, I am a bit worried about the recent decline in revenue growth, the company's relationship with Microsoft, and the deterioration of the business model. In my opinion, we may have to wait longer than expected for PERI to redesign its business model. With this in mind and considering recent stock returns after my last article, I decided to close my position in the stock. I do believe that the company is not that cheap right now. However, I would want to see whether I can buy more shares at around $8-$9 per share. I understand that other investors are maintaining their position because there are a lot of clever people inside PERI.

data.title: Measure Twice, Cut Once
data.description:
By Dasha Selivanova

U.S. large-cap growth has been outperforming this quarter, thanks in part to unusually strong contributions from a few single-stock winners. With higher dispersion among performance, market participants might conclude that it’s a good time to pick stocks or appoint a fund manager to do it for them. However, while the rewards for picking winners may be greater, it may not be any easier than usual to sort the wheat from the chaff.

Exhibit 1 shows the daily and cumulative relative QTD performance of the S&P 500® Growth as compared to the S&P 500. Much of the outperformance was concentrated in April and mid-May, but it has steadily outperformed over the period, for a cumulative 6.3% excess return.

While sectors traditionally associated with growth—such as Information Technology—also performed well during the period, Exhibit 2 shows that single-stock selections, rather than sectoral effects, made the greater contribution to growth’s outperformance. Overall, single-stock effects accounted for 3.8%, while sector effects totaled the remaining 2.5%.

On a related note, overall performance dispersion in growth names has been higher than usual. Exhibit 3 shows the average daily dispersion in the S&P 500 Growth YTD, as compared to the daily index dispersion since 1999. So far in 2025, annualized daily dispersion averaged 33%, far above the median and higher than 83% of historical days.

What does higher-than-average dispersion mean for stock pickers? A common assumption is that higher-dispersion environments favor active management, offering a higher degree of potential upside through astute stock selection. Examining the SPIVA® U.S. Year-End Scorecard results from 2003 to 2024 for the Large-Cap Growth fund category, we find that the range of manager outcomes has often risen and fallen in tandem with the prevalent levels of stock dispersion in the S&P 500 Growth. Specifically, Exhibit 4 compares the annual interquartile range among actively managed Large-Cap Growth funds to the average daily dispersion levels for the S&P 500 Growth by calendar year. During years characterized by unusually high dispersion, active funds often exhibited greater variability among their performances.

However, did this equate to higher rates of success among active funds? Opportunities may have been more common, but so was the risk associated with making the wrong selections. Again, SPIVA Scorecards bring this question into focus; wider dispersion did not increase the active manager outperformance rate. In fact, in the U.S. Large-Cap Growth active fund category, the average fund underperformance rate was 63% in the years of above-median dispersion and 55% in the years of below-median dispersion.

As the adage goes, “measuring twice” may prevent a costly mistake. Higher dispersion in growth may continue, along with some positive performance. Participating in winners could mean big rewards, but a hasty decision might be unwise, as shown by the historical SPIVA Scorecard results, 20 years of which are available here to assist market participants with their final cuts.

For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

data.title: Okta: Unmissable Value In Today's Market
data.description:
With the markets shifting their attention to the potential for Fed rate cuts and the narrative moving away from macroeconomic and geopolitical pressures, the S&P 500 is nearing YTD highs again, and for many stocks, particularly in the large-cap space, valuations are stretched to uncomfortable highs. To me, these conditions make it even more imperative for investors to rotate more of our portfolios into "growth at a reasonable price" stocks.

Okta (NASDAQ:OKTA), in my view, is one of the best plays here. Okta is a terrific example of how quickly a former market darling can transition into a penalty-box stock. Though Okta is up more than 20% since the start of January, the stock has only barely shaken off losses in 2024, owing to sales execution missteps that plagued the company's growth rates and net retention rates. With evidence of much stronger sales execution this year, however, Okta is well poised to rebound.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Okta in March, when the company was still trading around ~$100 per share. Since then, Okta has slid somewhat on its recent failure to boost its full-year outlook. At the same time, however, I appreciate the fact that Okta is making sharp progress at fixing its go-to-market flukes while at the same time driving major improvements in its operating margins. With very undemanding valuation multiples relative to its TAM and to its growth rates versus peers, I am reiterating my buy opinion here.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Okta, here is what I view as the core long-term bull thesis for this stock:

  • Best in class identity management platform. Okta is broadly considered to be the leader in the single sign-on category, helping companies to securely manage employee profiles and logins across both internal applications and cloud-based applications.
  • Huge $80 billion TAM. Okta estimates its global TAM at $80 billion, indicating that the company's current ~$3 billion in annualized revenue is less than 4% penetrated into its global opportunity. Though all attention has shifted to AI, we also can't forget the tailwinds of more and more companies migrating apps away from on-prem and into the cloud, which is requiring coordination in single sign-on from the likes of Okta.
  • Excellent RPO growth. Okta's remaining performance obligations, which is a primary measure of backlog for many enterprise software companies, is growing more quickly than its revenue - which signals that the company may be able to achieve revenue acceleration in the near term.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Okta manages to achieve low double digits revenue growth stacked on top of high 20s pro forma operating margins, which positions the company extremely close to the Rule of 40 (a commonly used measure of growth and profitability balance for software companies that adds revenue growth to operating margin).

Despite Okta's strengths, the company's penalty box status since last year (owing to choppy sales execution) has cast the pall of discounted valuation multiples on this stock, which still persist today. At current share prices just under $100, Okta trades at a market cap of $17.18 billion. After we net off the $2.73 billion of cash and $858 million of convertible debt on Okta's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $15.31 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Okta ending in January 2026), the company has maintained its guidance at $2.85-$2.86 billion in revenue, or 9-10% y/y growth. Its 27% FCF margin also implies ~$772 million in FCF

This positions Okta's valuation at:

  • 5.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 19.8x EV/FY25 revenue

When we stack up Okta against other software companies that are expected to grow revenue in the low double digits, we find that there is at least a one-turn discount to peers like Workday:

Stay long here and hold out for a broader rebound in Okta.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Okta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Okta's revenue grew 12% y/y to $688 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $680 million (+10% y/y) while decelerating only one point from 13% y/y growth in Q4.

Off the bat, we do note it was a rather mixed quarter for sales execution. One of the metrics that Okta continues to disappoint on is net retention rates, which weakened one point further to 106%. This being said, Okta isn't alone in the software industry in facing waning net retention rates: as companies slow headcount growth and some even go to the extreme of executing sharp layoffs, companies like Okta that price their products on a per-seat basis have been under more pressure to keep up the same pace of expansion.

That being said, the company notes that it is building up a healthy pipeline, and the recent segmentation of the company's sales teams on a product basis (between Okta and its subsidiary Auth0 which it acquired in 2021) has yielded positive results. Per CFO Brett Tighe's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We entered the first quarter with the previously announced realignment of our go-to-market team, which further specialized our sales force into Okta sellers and Auth0 sellers. While it's still too early to judge the overall success of this realignment, we're encouraged by some of the early signals in the Q1 results. In particular, Auth0 performed quite well following a record Q4. We were also pleased to see pipeline strengthening throughout March and April.

We remain confident that increased go-to-market specialization will yield long-term benefits for Okta and our customers. Adding to our confidence is the recent performance we're seeing in the parts of our business that had already been specialized. At the beginning of last year, we moved the team focused on the U.S. SMB market to a hunter-farmer model. That team performed well in Q1 and underscores how specialization can drive improvements over time.

Another area that has been specialized for some time is the U.S. public sector vertical, which has been an area of strength over the last few years. The strength has been a direct result of Okta's strategic commitment and investments in the U.S. public sector. Our public sector team had a strong Q1 as two of our top three and four of our top 10 deals were in the public sector, including the federal deal we called out in our posted commentary."

Evidence of Okta's improving deal momentum is evident in the company's remaining performance obligations (essentially a measure of backlog). Total RPO grew 21% y/y to $4.08 billion (indicating a slew of multi-year deals), while current RPO (which is the portion that will convert to revenue within one year) grew 14% y/y; decelerating only one point from 15% y/y RPO growth in Q4. Both of these metrics are well above revenue growth, and a healthy signal that Okta's growth has a good chance of holding at its current low double digit pace, if not even accelerate (recall that Okta's full-year guidance has growth dipping to the 9-10% range for the full year, which I find conservative with room for upside revision).

The company has also maintained excellent cost discipline. As shown in the chart below, Okta's pro forma operating margins rose 5 points y/y to 27%, while nominal pro forma operating income dollars grew 38% y/y to $184 million.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that investors need to keep in mind with Okta. In particular, we should monitor closely how AI impacts use cases for Okta. As companies like Salesforce begin to introduce agentic AI products, login by human employees may eventually give way to more tasks being performed directly by agentic AI - and in this space, companies like SailPoint (SAIL) have built up more of a brand around securely provisioning data access to AI personas. We should also be wary of the macro environment: despite recent optimism in the stock market, big companies are slashing headcount, which is a direct hit to Okta's net revenue retention rates.

That being said, I think these risks are already quite well captured into the stock's below-market ~5x forward revenue multiple. Take advantage of the opportunity to buy Okta while it's still cheap.

data.title: VanEck Mid-June Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin sets new highs and holds strong above $100K as Ordinals fade and onchain activity slows. Equity markets reward treasury-heavy BTC proxies over operational miners.

Please note that VanEck has exposure to bitcoin.

Three key takeaways for mid-May to mid-June:

  1. Price Resilience in a Volatile Macro Backdrop: Bitcoin (BTC) set new all-time highs above $110K, though geopolitical tension briefly pushed BTC as low as $98K on June 22nd, marking a six-week low.
  2. Onchain Activity Slows: Transaction volume and fee revenue declined, with Ordinals volumes hitting year-to-date lows, highlighting Bitcoin’s growing reliance on off-chain adoption.
  3. Treasury Plays Outperform… for Now: Metaplanet’s (OTC:MTPLF) +125% rally highlights recent investor preference for balance sheet BTC exposure over operational mining models, though leadership could shift with some valuations looking stretched.

Bitcoin’s Price Action

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Borrowing Rates are Near YTD Lows Despite All-Time High Prices

Bitcoin quietly carved new all-time highs this month, reaching nearly $112K on May 22 and trading above $110K again on June 9. Yet, in the wake of escalating U.S.-Iran tensions over the weekend, BTC briefly fell to ~$98K, its lowest level in over a month, before stabilizing back above $100K.

Despite this volatility, 30-day moving averages remain elevated at ~$107K. Interestingly, borrowing rates have cooled to 7%, about 50% lower than at the start of 2025, indicating neutral market sentiment and increased stability.

While future conditions remain uncertain, Bitcoin’s evolving role as both a high-beta tech proxy and a macro hedge may allow it to benefit across multiple scenarios. In peace, improved risk appetite could favor high-growth assets like BTC. In conflict, rising fiscal deficits and currency concerns may bolster the asset’s appeal among reserve-seeking investors.

Bitcoin’s Market Cap Dominance Hit Multi-Year Highs in June

Bitcoin Dominance and Institutional Flows

Bitcoin’s market dominance rose another 1% in June, setting a new 30-day moving average high for this cycle. This continued rise reflects Bitcoin’s strengthening role as the preferred crypto asset among institutional investors, even as altcoins remain largely trapped in speculative, retail-driven narratives.

Since the 2021–2022 altcoin cycle, Bitcoin has reclaimed nearly all market share gains made by other crypto assets. The momentum in dominance supports the view that Bitcoin has matured into a digital store-of-value -- functionally “digital gold”-- for both public and private institutions.

Unlike prior cycles, investors today can access leveraged BTC exposure through public equities. Strategy (MSTR), for example, offers deep liquidity and capital efficiency, while smaller entrants like Semler Scientific (SMLR) provide high-volatility trading opportunities. These structures appear to absorb speculative demand that might have previously flowed into altcoins.

While ETH and SOL treasury strategies show early promise, Bitcoin continues to command the lion’s share of institutional flows, supported by its dominant liquidity, simple narrative, and macro positioning. Altcoins, by contrast, have struggled to attract sustained inflows, with market activity largely rotational and retail-driven. As a result, Bitcoin appears to be pulling in net new capital, while altcoins operate in a more insular environment, reinforcing BTC’s status as the foundation of institutional crypto exposure.

Onchain Performance: Activity Slows as Offchain Adoption Grows

Daily Bitcoin Transactions and Fees Hovered Near YTD Lows in June

While market-facing indicators such as price action, futures, flows, and institutional positioning remain supportive, Bitcoin’s on-chain metrics point to softening activity. Over the past 30 days:

  • The Bitcoin network processed an average of 364,000 transactions per day, down 8% month-over-month and 42% year-over-year.
  • Daily fee revenue averaged $641K, down 57% year-to-date.
  • In contrast, block rewards earned by miners remain elevated at approximately $45M per day, assuming 144 blocks/day at $100K per BTC.
  • This means that, on an annualized basis, transaction fees account for just 1.4% of the estimated $16.4B in yearly block rewards, a stark mismatch between network usage and price performance.

This disconnect may persist, especially as Bitcoin adoption increasingly occurs offchain through financial products like ETPs and corporate treasuries, rather than through native blockchain activity. Looking ahead, the scheduled block reward halvings in 2028 and 2032 will further reduce miner revenue, raising structural questions about the long-term sustainability of security incentives if fee generation doesn’t materially recover.

Ordinals ("Bitcoin NFTs") Activity Fell To 2025 Lows in June

Ordinals Cool Off

A major factor behind Bitcoin’s weaker on-chain metrics is the collapse in activity related to Ordinals, which allowed users to inscribe digital content onto individual Satoshis—effectively enabling Bitcoin-native NFTs.

After launching in early 2023, Ordinals briefly revitalized Bitcoin’s base layer, bringing in unprecedented retail demand. Transaction volumes surged, fees spiked, and Bitcoin—which historically lacked the smart contract capabilities of chains like Ethereum (ETH-USD) and Solana (SOL-USD)—saw renewed relevance as an application layer.

From late 2023 through early 2024, Ordinals helped narrow the perceived gap between Bitcoin and more expressive chains by enabling digital collectibles, meme assets, and experimental DeFi primitives on BTC. But in June, activity collapsed: transaction volumes fell 39% month-over-month, marking new 2025 lows and the weakest performance since the latest wave of interest began in late 2024.

The decline reflects both cooling speculative interest and structural challenges in sustaining high-fee on-chain activity. While the protocol was an important moment for Bitcoin’s cultural evolution, its volatility underscores the difficulty of building persistent user demand on a chain not optimized for programmability.

Bitcoin Pure-Play Stocks

Metaplanet (+125%)

Metaplanet stood out as a top performer among Bitcoin pure-plays this month, up 125% MoM. The company announced three purchases of Bitcoin: On May 19th, June 2nd, and June 16th, Metaplanet acquired 1,004, 1,088, and 1,112 Bitcoin, respectively, bringing its total holdings to 10,000 BTC. The company also announced its 2025-2027 Bitcoin Plan, targeting 210,000 BTC by 2027. While we flag that Metaplanet’s NAV Premium to basic shares (~7.1x) remains elevated compared to Strategy’s (~1.8x) and Semler Scientific’s (~1.1x), we recognize the company’s dominance as a Bitcoin treasury company in the Japanese and broader Asian markets, in addition to certain tax and regulatory advantages it offers over spot Bitcoin.

Strategy (-11%)

In late May, Strategy announced its latest Bitcoin acquisition, adding 4,020 to bring its total to 580,250 BTC. On June 6th, Strategy priced a $980M public offering of 10.00% Series A Perpetual Preferred Stock ('STRD'), with proceeds earmarked for further Bitcoin acquisitions and general corporate purposes. The non-cumulative preferred shares, structured to pay quarterly cash dividends when declared, represent Strategy’s latest innovation in capital structure to scale BTC exposure without issuing common equity. While MSTR’s 1M performance was negative, we note that its YTD (+28%) performance still largely outpaces Bitcoin’s (+7%), and that its NAV premium suggests more downside protection than Metaplanet’s.

CleanSpark (-7%)

CleanSpark (CLSK) doubled its BTC treasury year-over-year, now holding 12,502 BTC. In May, it produced 694 BTC, selling only 293.5. While CleanSpark holds more BTC than Metaplanet, its market cap is only one-third as large, suggesting either a steep discount on miners or a premium on treasuries.

Notably, CleanSpark has avoided equity dilution since November 2024, a rare feat in the mining space, and continues to scale through localized energy strategies. Still, the market’s broader view on mining economics remains skeptical.

Marathon Digital (-12%)

Despite having the highest energized hash rate (58.3 EH/s) and the second-largest BTC treasury (49,179 BTC), Marathon fell 12% MoM, underperforming other pure-plays. While the company has begun building out its own infrastructure, about 45% of its operational hashrate still comes from hosted arrangements, which can limit margins and strategic flexibility, especially when compared to fully integrated miners. Marathon’s past reliance on third-party hosting and its history of equity dilution continue to weigh on investor sentiment. That overhang could persist unless the company transitions more fully to a self-operated model or demonstrates progress in diversifying its revenue base beyond core mining.

data.title: AMG TimesSquare Mid Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG TimesSquare Mid Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:TQMIX) (Class I) returned -3.48% for the first quarter of 2025, compared with -7.12% for the benchmark, the Russell Midcap ® Growth Index. For the 12 months ending March 31, 2025, the Fund returned −2.74%, while the benchmark returned 3.57%.

Quarterly Update

Expectations for businesses and markets were broadly positive as 2025 began. Hopes for a pro-business, lighter regulatory environment lifted global markets in January. However, delays and uncertainties regarding U.S. policies tempered markets in February, and the U.S. pulled back sharply in March (to say nothing about the early part of April).

  • In the first quarter, broad-based weakness of U.S. equities (-5% return for the Russell 3000® Index) lagged overseas markets ( 7% for the MSCI EAFE Index and 3% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index).

Even before the market's tariff tantrum at the start of April, there were inflationary pressures. Global Purchasing Manager Indices (PMIs) showed moderating New Order rates and higher Prices Paid/Output for manufacturing and service activities.

  • While value indices outperformed growth, both Growth and Value had positive returns among global risk factors. It was the negative showing from Beta that overwhelmed everything else.

Fund Update

Amidst this negative market environment, the Fund outperformed the Russell Midcap ® Growth Index in the first quarter.

In the communication services sector, we generally prefer entertainment or media-related companies that either benefit from providing advertisers with a different target audience or offer related services. Gaining 7% was the image-based social media network for discovery and planning, Pinterest, Inc. (PINS) Pinterest exceeded expectations for revenues and earnings thanks to better user engagement and improved advertising placements, which led to higher monetization levels. The numbers of monthly active users also increased by over 10% (to 553 mm).

Our preferences in the consumer-oriented sectors lean toward value-oriented or specialty retailers, franchise models, premium brands, or support services for other consumer companies. One benefit was the specialty retailer and supplier of auto parts, tools, supplies, and accessories, O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY). A steady increase in same-store sales led O'Reilly's revenues above expectations. The company's management tempered its 2025 guidance, which seemed well-telegraphed given broader consumer caution, and its shares climbed by 21%.

While recent results were in-line with expectations, Ross Stores (ROST) provided forward guidance that was less than anticipated. The operator of discount apparel and home fashion outlets anticipates a slow start to the year attributable in part to the new CEO's understandable caution amidst present economic conditions. That marked down Ross's shares by −15%.

Warehouse club operator BJ's Wholesale (BJ) bested expectations for revenues and growth with better same-store sales growth. BJ's forward guidance was in-line with projections and the company plans an accelerated pace of new store openings over the next two years. Its shares climbed by 28% this quarter.

In the financials sector we tend to avoid banks that face credit deterioration or rising deposit costs, preferring either asset managers, specialized insurance companies, or financial technology providers. On the plus side there was an 8% gain from Allstate Corporation (ALL), which provides personal property and casualty insurance, life insurance, as well as retirement and investment products. Revenues and earnings were higher than anticipated, propelled by underlying loss ratio beats across Homeowners and auto insurance as well as lower than expected catastrophe losses. Notably, the company highlighted that it expects policies-in-force to grow in 2025.

Offsetting that was a - 24% showing from private equity manager TPG Inc. (TPG) While distributable earnings were above expectations, the underlying fee-related revenues were below. Some of that disconnect came from the consolidation of recently acquired Angelo Gordon in TPG's numbers, which will be measured on an apples-to-apples basis going forward.

Elsewhere, we benefited from a 12% gain from the online brokerage platform Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD) At the start of the year, increased monthly trading volumes for options, significant gains in equity trading, and both were eclipsed by the growth of customers trading cryptocurrencies. Mid-quarter, Robinhood reported surging revenue and earnings by exceeding $1B of quarterly revenues for the first time, and its premium Robinhood Gold membership ranks swelled.

Our preferences among health care stocks are those companies providing novel therapies for unmet needs that deserve premium pricing, or specialized service providers. We were rewarded with a 52% return from Intra-Cellular Therapies. A developer of small molecule treatments for neurological disorders, we added Intra-Cellular to the Fund in December. In January, the company agreed to an acquisition offer from Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and we sold our shares in February.

Cencora, Inc. (COR), which distributes pharmaceutical and medical products to pharmacies, hospitals, and other health care providers, saw its price improve by 24%. Cencora's revenues and earnings each exceeded expectations, and management further increased forward guidance to reflect its business momentum. Later in the quarter, Cencora again lifted its outlook though that did not include future benefits from a recent acquisition.

Many of our industrials positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, equipment enabling automation and efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services. Case in point was Waste Connections (WCN), which collects, transfers, recycles, and disposes of waste for municipalities and businesses in the U.S. and Canada. Revenues exceeded expectations though earnings fell shy of them given the impacts of foreign exchange and lower prices for recycled cardboard. The resilience of Waste Connections' business within the current economic landscape contributed to a 14% increase in its stock price.

Pulling back by −19% was EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME), which provides construction and operational services for mechanical and electrical systems to a broad range of commercial, industrial, utility, and institutional customers. Although revenues were less than expected, earnings were better, and the company increased earnings guidance along with its share repurchase authorization for 2025. The underlying business trends remained strong, though there were some market concerns that EMCOR's activities tied to data center construction would slow. Previously, we had trimmed our position somewhat, seeing early signals of that sentiment shift. As EMCOR's price fell, we began adding back given the strong demand the company saw.

On a positive note, there was Verisk Analytics (vrs), the property/casualty insurance risk information and analysis provider. The company reported revenues and earnings that exceeded expectations with steady organic growth. Management was upbeat regarding strategic initiatives that should improve sales momentum and pricing, though was conservative with its initial guidance for 2025. Combined, that gave Verisk's shares an 8% lift. Among the wide variety of information technology companies, we prefer critical system providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve productivity or efficiency for their clients, and others that are growing their shares of corporate IT budgets.

Jumping ahead by 9% was JFrog Ltd. (FROG), which provides systems to manage and speed up the release of software updates from developers to users. We added to our position in February with the view that the wider creation or adoption of GenAI applications for businesses would generate greater activity volumes for JFrog. That outlook was supported by the company's subsequent report. Revenues, earnings, and forward guidance were all higher than anticipated as JFrog closed several multi-million-dollar deals along with higher rates of mid and smaller sized contracts. After that report, we met with JFrog's management and discussed its initial successes cross selling additional security services. Thus, when its price retreated in March, we bought additional shares in JFrog.

Slipping by -13% was Gartner, Inc. (IT), which offers independent and objective research and analysis on information technology, computer hardware, software, and communications. While revenues and earnings were better than expected thanks to accelerating growth in contract values, Gartner's outlook for the year was less than anticipated. From our experience, we have seen Gartner begin its fiscal years conservatively only to steadily improve its results, so we added to our position. There was weakness in Gartner's shares later in the quarter when federal government cost cutting was projected to affect about 4% of Gartner's revenues (though we believe that was a price overreaction).

We had a different view on Teradyne, Inc. (TER), a leading producer of testing and measurement equipment for semiconductors and other complex electronic systems. Although its revenues and earnings were higher than forecasted, Teradyne's management lowered its guidance for the full year. The company had presented an optimistic picture of the broad industry outlook, but was unable to translate that to its own sales expectations. On a later analyst day, Teradyne again tempered its outlook, and we exited the position as it declined by −29% for the partial quarter.

To note that the global trade outlook and its ramifications across economies are "fluid" (the operative term for many current evaluations), seems an understatement. We see companies girding for greater turbulence before the waters calm. The incorporation of a weak first quarter and the Trump administration's "shock and awe" tariff policy unveiled in early April led the U.S. Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) to reduce their U.S. and global gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecasts. All of this has created significant price swings recently. In a few instances, we may find stocks that have so dramatically oversold that they warrant us adding to those positions. At the same time, we recognize the potential for additional rapid changes in government policies, so we are cautious about overreacting. As long term investors, our approach has historically been more resilient to these types of market upheavals, and that investment style has not changed. As always, we are available for any questions you might have.

data.description:
Markets rallied as Washington and Beijing agreed not to escalate. Labor Day became the new line in the sand, and Section 899 was blindfolded and tied to a post, prompting traders to believe diplomacy might outrun duty hikes.

Level Change 6/27/25 (%)

+1.0 Dow

+0.5 Nasdaq

+0.4 Nasdaq 100

+0.5 S&P 500

+0.3 S&P 400

+0.2 S&P 600

Stocks shook off a midday stupor for a final-hour dash, closing the week on a strong note. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq set fresh records, with tariff bears and oil bulls nowhere to be found - last seen muttering about mispriced risk before disappearing into the tall grass.

Start with trade: The US and China dusted off their Geneva handshake and affirmed a framework to implement May’s grand bargain away from trade-war maximalism. Washington will loosen a few screws, while Beijing speeds up exports of the minerals that make modern life scroll.

Canada, on the other hand, got shoved back down the stairs. President Trump nixed talks over Ottawa’s digital service taxes and promised to reveal Canada’s new tariff rate within a week. Odds favor higher - and a fresh nickname. Maple Moochers, obviously.

Treasury Secretary Bessent played good cop, stretching the July 9 trade deal deadline to Labor Day. He also urged Congress to yank Section 899 from the big, beautiful bill - a Trumpian tool for retaliatory tax tantrums. “Greater certainty for the global economy,” he said, in a statement that sounded like Oppenheimer praising the disappearance of nuclear weapons.

Elsewhere in the House of Numbers and Nonsense, inflation refused to cooperate with forecasts. Core PCE rose 0.2% in May, a tick hotter than the expected 0.1%. Personal income and spending both fell. The consumer, it seems, is finally running out of rope, or at least choosing not to lasso anything new with it.

The Fed may not mind. Minneapolis’s Kashkari reiterated his call for two cuts this year, with the first possibly in September - unless tariffs hit like a hammer instead of a flyswatter. Another fine entry in the two-handed economics genre: it’ll be fine, unless it isn’t.

Which brings us to Nike (NKE +15.2%), poster child for not-great news that isn’t quite as bad as feared. Revenue fell 12%, tariffs will cost it $1B, and margins are set to shrink - but guidance for next quarter beat expectations. That was all Wall Street needed. If the swoosh can stomach the storm, maybe the rest of retail won’t drown.

And that was the day: China got coaxed, Canada got iced, consumers got cautious, and Nike got credit for running in place.
